channel.title: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD)
channel.description: Daily Feed (Last 48 Hours, Updated 9 AM BD) generated by script

item.title: Groundhog Day: Iran War Edition
item.description: It’s an open question if negotiators can break the repetitive cycle engulfing the war.

item.title: Tentative U.S.-Iran Cease-Fire Extension Deal Awaits Trump’s Approval
item.description: Tehran’s leadership has also not publicly signed off on the agreement yet.

item.title: No Commodity Is Safe From the Iran War
item.description: From Diet Coke to condoms, the world’s supply chains have faced surprising downstream disruptions.

item.title: The Iran War Has Only Worsened Sudan’s Conflict
item.description: The country is uniquely vulnerable to regional shocks.

item.title: Erdogan Is Forcibly Designing His Own Opposition
item.description: Turkey is moving from repressing the opposition to reshaping it.

item.title: What the Latest China-Russia Summit Means for Ukraine
item.description: The Putin-Xi joint statement is not merely a statement of bilateral friendship. It is a war-era manifesto.

item.title: Cubans Abandon the American Dream
item.description: As conditions on the island worsen, people are leaving—just not for the United States.

item.title: When Buddhist Robes Meet Sri Lankan Law
item.description: Recently, the chief prelate or custodian of eight sacred sites in Sri Lanka was arrested on charges of sexually assaulting and raping a child. He is out on bail.

item.title: Mosquitoes seem to be getting over insect repellent
item.description: They learn to associate it with food

item.title: The Double China Shock: How Beijing Is Disrupting Both Developing and Advanced Economies
item.description: China intends to keep making low-tech goods alongside its push into advanced technology. That's bad news for advanced and developing economies alike.

item.title: Bibi’s Manichean Politics
item.description: Over three decades, Netanyahu’s pivot to divisive electioneering has paid off.

item.title: Rethinking the ‘Absolute Bar’ on Scheduled Caste Status in India
item.description: By tying caste recognition to religion, the law risks excluding those who continue to face the same discrimination it seeks to remedy.

item.title: Americans Can’t Afford Trump’s Economy
item.description: Prices are rising faster than wages in the US, and President Donald Trump’s policies are a key reason why. Now is the time for the Democratic Party to advance a credible and specific “affordability platform” that addresses the biggest culprits, from tariffs and energy prices to mass deportation of immigrants.

item.title: Transporting Oil to China by Rail Will Not Solve Iran’s Export Headache
item.description: The overland route presents a limited lifeline for Tehran, but cannot substitute for sea-based oil trade.

item.title: The Manus Fallout Highlights Structural Problems in China’s Industrial Policy Ecosystem
item.description: China wants to welcome foreign investment, but its red lines are shifting. That’s not an attractive proposition for many companies.

item.title: The Madman Strikes Back
item.description: Nixon’s theory on the power of unpredictability didn’t account for an actual madman adversary.

item.title: Friedrich Merz Doesn’t Understand the EU
item.description: Germany’s chancellor keeps complaining about the source of the continent’s power.

item.title: Trump Should Just Admit He Screwed Up
item.description: The Iran war was obviously a mistake. Why not say so?

item.title: Australia’s View of the Evolving Quad
item.description: Canberra’s position – that Indo-Pacific stability depends not only on military deterrence, but also on the protection of trade flows, energy supplies, critical technologies, and economic sovereignty – has moved to the fore.

item.title: China’s Navy Is Shifting Pressure Beyond the Taiwan Strait
item.description: China did not launch a large-scale, Taiwan-specific military exercise after the Trump-Xi meeting. But that doesn’t mean the PLAN has been inactive.

item.title: Britain’s Center Cannot Hold
item.description: A combination of globalization, deindustrialization, and other forces destroyed the old model of party politics that long prevailed in developed democracies. Nowhere is this more apparent than in the United Kingdom, where voters are angry and demanding radical solutions that no political leader can actually deliver.

item.title: Why the world needs more franchises
item.description: From pizza to Pilates, franchises mint millionaires and make customers happy

item.title: The imperial vision of Ethiopia’s Abiy Ahmed
item.description: The prime minister’s ambitions threaten both his country and the Horn of Africa

item.title: llliberal leaders in mainland South-East Asia revamp their regimes
item.description: They are doing better than their more democratic counterparts

item.title: Indonesia’s erratic president grabs the country’s commodity exports
item.description: Facing a tumbling currency, Prabowo Subianto announces a vast new agency to sell resources to the world

item.title: Bowing to online fury, China’s censors ban a prize-winning film
item.description: Both nationalists and the manosphere resented its portrayal of their country

item.title: A coalmine explosion lays bare China’s two-speed economy
item.description: As its industries of the future take off, old ones still flout basic safety procedures

item.title: Are Angelenos angry enough to elect an insurgent as mayor?
item.description: Spencer Pratt tests the limits of grievance politics in a blue city

item.title: Why can’t Elon Musk do for politics what he’s done for industry?
item.description: So much for advancing civilisation

item.title: The refugees Donald Trump wants are white and middle-class
item.description: Afrikaners are welcome. Pretty much no one else is

item.title: Meet the Republicans defying Donald Trump
item.description: Conservatives with nothing left to lose are bucking the president

item.title: The weekly cartoon
Politics
Business
How the boomers screwed Europe
item.description: The huge rock ’n’ roll generation is sticking its few kids with the bill

item.title: Leo’s first encyclical attacks technological messianism
item.description: The Pope warns against replacing humans with AI, though he seems to use it himself

item.title: Europe’s superyacht-builders hit choppy waters
item.description: Fewer plutocrats want floating palaces

item.title: How should bosses talk about AI?
item.description: Employees are being asked to embrace a technology that causes fear

item.title: The Gulf war makes devastating oil spills more likely
item.description: It would also make them harder to handle

item.title: Congo’s response to Ebola is late and chaotic
item.description: It is hard to think of a more difficult place to stem an epidemic

item.title: The Putin-Xi Summit and the Asymmetry of the China-Russia Partnership
item.description: While both states share dissatisfaction with the Western-led international order and seek a more multipolar system, the summit revealed important differences.

item.title: Will the EU Develop a Pragmatic Strategy Toward Afghanistan?
item.description: Afghanistan’s path to international recognition and fuller participation in the global community depends, in part, on the approach the European Union chooses to take.

item.title: Reading Between China’s Red Lines
item.description: Chinese President Xi Jinping’s blunt warning to US President Donald Trump about Taiwan was clearly intended to sharpen the distinction between this ultimatum and the many others Xi has issued. But expanding the number of non-negotiable positions reduces the space for effective diplomacy and raises the risk of accidental conflict.

item.title: The Pope Should Have Gone Further on AI
item.description: Pope Leo XIV has made waves with his first encyclical, in which he describes the development and deployment of new AI tools as a profound threat to human dignity. If anything, he has not gone far enough in pressing the question of what the technology is being designed to do.

item.title: How East Asia should respond to its China shock
item.description: As they deindustrialise, Taiwan, South Korea and Japan must reform

item.title: China is quietly making rural migrants’ lives easier
item.description: The notorious hukou system is being relaxed. Marx would approve

item.title: Without fanfare, China is making rural migrants’ lives easier
item.description: The notorious hukou system is being relaxed. Marx would approve

item.title: How to tax businesses in orbit and beyond
item.description: As the space business grows, so will offplanet finance

item.title: Japan’s beloved Indian restaurants are under threat
item.description: An unexpected casualty of the country’s immigration backlash

item.title: Everything is going right for India’s richest man
item.description: Gautam Adani is on a winning streak

item.title: BP cares too much about feelings and not enough about performance
item.description: The defenestration of its chairman is but the latest example

item.title: America and Iran are getting close to a deal. Or not
item.description: A bewildering week brought both frenzied negotiations and renewed American strikes

item.title: Smart tech is making war a dumber choice
item.description: Smaller, weaker countries can defend themselves more easily with cheap, deadly kit

item.title: Why the Pakistan-China Partnership Remains Resilient
item.description: In Beijing, Islamabad endorsed “Xi’s vision” and global initiatives. China endorsed Pakistan’s position on the Kashmir dispute.

item.title: What Happened at Htan Shauk Khan in Myanmar’s Rakhine State on May 2, 2024?
item.description: The Arakan Army was accused of slaughtering civilians. But residents say that air strikes and shelling on the village caused the fatalities.

item.title: Why China Never Captured the Indian Imagination
item.description: Their customs, norms, language, food and music are different. They have radically different approaches to politics, ideas, and religion too.

item.title: A Welsh Reporter on Myanmar’s Frontlines
item.description: Lorcan Lovett speaks about the plight facing Rohingya refugees and the current state of the country's civil war.

item.title: The Coming Crisis of NATO Deterrence
item.description: Nuclear guarantees cannot replace U.S. forces in Europe.

item.title: Trump’s Least Bad Option in Iran
item.description: The logic behind a limited deal.

item.title: The Strategic Corridor Turning Singapore Into China’s Indispensable Partner
item.description: Beijing’s New International Land-Sea Trade Corridor is emerging as a strategic supply line for Beijing – and Singapore helped design it.

item.title: ICC Announces November 30 as Start Date for Trial of Former Philippine President
item.description: Rodrigo Duterte faces three counts of crimes against humanity involving the murders of at least ⁠76 people during violent anti-drug crackdowns.

item.title: Is 2026 the Year Thailand’s Economy Bounces Back?
item.description: The country's export-dependent economy has been hit hard by geopolitical turmoil, trade tensions, and the chronic instability of Thai politics.

item.title: Singaporean Foreign Minister Winds Up Working Visit to North Korea
item.description: The visit, which coincided with the 50th anniversary of relations between the two countries, came as part of a tour that also included stops in China and South Korea.

item.title: A Textbook Debate: Why Russian Historians Told Kyrgyz Historians to Ditch the Term ‘Colonialism’
item.description: If legitimacy is tied to history, then exactly how that history is interpreted matters beyond conference rooms packed with historians.

item.title: Understanding Cambodia’s Scam Economy, with Jacob Sims
item.description: The scam economy is an industrialized system that merges cyber fraud with human trafficking and money laundering, all thriving under political protection.

item.title: Where Does Bangladesh’s Once Dominant Awami League Stand Today?
item.description: The former ruling party, once a mainstay of Bangladeshi politics, has largely disappeared from public political life. But its leaders remain defiant.

item.title: What’s Driving Anti-Qing Sentiment in Contemporary China?
item.description: A patriotic film backfired because a growing number of Han Chinese don’t see the Manchu-origin Qing dynasty as a part of their history.

item.title: China’s Rise and the Challenge to US Maritime Security
item.description: Insights from James R. Holmes.
